deltas.product_description:
We’re seeing this a lot in the automation space, and honestly, I’ve been guilty of it too.

Most automation agencies make one mistake, they try to sell automation.
But selling automation often adds cost before it adds value.

Because in the early stages, most of what automation promises can actually be done manually, faster, cheaper, and with more context.

The real need for automation begins when manual work becomes the bottleneck, when scaling becomes painful, repetitive, and error-prone.

That’s when automation stops being a “nice-to-have” and becomes an enabler of growth.

So instead of trying to sell automation, we build it where the pain is real, where teams are already doing the work, and scaling it manually no longer makes sense.

At CodingRippler, that’s how we think about automation,

Change is Coming: The Army Fitness Test Evolves

Starting June 1, 2025, the Army is implementing the new Army Fitness Test (AFT) - a five-event assessment built to meet the demands of modern warfighting.

• AFT becomes the official test of record
• 5 events: Deadlift, Push-Up, Sprint-Drag-Carry, Plank, 2-Mile Run

prediction.review_text:
I've been reflecting on the concept of 'unit' in unit testing, especially as we navigate the complexities of refactoring. It's fascinating how the definition has shifted from a focus on meaningful behavior to often being tied to classes or methods. 🤔 

I believe we should be testing at the level of functional behavior—grouping classes that deliver capabilities together. This approach not only protects us during refactoring but also keeps our tests relevant and effective. 

I'm genuinely curious about your experiences with this. What patterns have worked for your teams, especially at scale? Let's share insights and help each other refine our testing strategies! #UnitTesting #TDD #SoftwareDevelopment

actual.review_text:
My experience has been that the role of unit tests is to both model the real-world scenarios you'd encounter in production and to test the framework. If a change to the framework occurs you need to change the tests too, those should be fairly straightforward and simple, and safe to regenerate with AI. They provide the least value, guarding only against minor changes that don't alter function signatures or ifaces, they're there to help you when someone changed a label, added an if to some internals, etc.

Situations where "unit" is what should be used as placeholder the meaning of functionality, which may sometimes require some mocking of other subsystems external to your code in order to keep focus on the outcome you're trying to model, are far more valuable and require good separation of concerns.
My exp has been that the minimal API-mocking ones are easy to implement and usually get the time they require, while the latter (which ironically provide far better value) get deferred and may get attention when someone that spent 2 months on some complicated investigation decides to invest that time without approval. I don't really care what people call unit, but I care that the functional tests exist. And a lot of times they don't.

deltas.product_description:
A question for my network — genuinely curious about your experience.

First, let me be clear where I stand: I'm a believer in the full testing pyramid. Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, contract testing, property-based testing, mutation testing, performance testing, resilience testing, chaos engineering — all of it. Each layer catches different things, and I've seen the value at every level.

My question is narrower. It's about what we mean by "unit" in unit testing.

To be fair, I see value in the class-level, mock-heavy approach — especially for coordination layers where you genuinely care that the right services get called in the right order. It's also useful as a design feedback tool: painful mock setup often signals architectural problems. I'm not proposing we abandon it. I'm proposing we layer it with behavior-level testing. Ideally, I'd want both — full coverage at every layer of the pyramid, including both styles of unit testing. The only tests I'd skip are the truly redundant ones (I once saw a team unit test a pure delegation layer that was already exercised by every other layer — that's just noise).

Saying this, I'm not sure the way most teams do unit testing actually helps when it matters most (as a non-regression safety net during refactoring/upgrades that is there to support & encourage such ongoing refactoring).

Here's what I mean. The moment I need tests the most is during refactoring — when I'm restructuring code but want behavior to stay the same. Tests should be my safety net.

But if my tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, then refactoring *breaks the tests*. Not because behavior changed, but because the internal wiring changed. Now I'm rewriting tests and code at the same time, and I've lost my independent witness. I'm not refactoring anymore — I'm rewriting with hope.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.

prediction.review_text: Should we be open about our salaries? 🤔 In many industries, discussing pay is still considered taboo, yet transparency can play a crucial role in closing wage gaps and fostering fair negotiations. As someone who values inclusivity and equitable hiring practices, I believe sharing salary information can empower us all. 💪 Let's break the silence and promote a culture of openness! What are your thoughts? Vote in the poll and share your insights in the comments! #SalaryTransparency #EquityInTech #CareerGrowth

actual.review_text: Transparency should be implemented at company level and it should be presented as brackets, not individual salaries. Based on personal merit and impact on company any individual progresses towards the higher part of the bracket until promoted. The first place to start with this transparency should be in job ads though, many times the lack of this transparency encourages personal statistics made by asking friends who don't really do the job you're applying for or searching inaccurate websites causing many people to make bad decisions of either asking too much or too little in interviews. Transparency can also be a motivating factor, to know what one could earn more by doing some specific type of development can make some people have clear career paths early on, provided they have the dedication, ability and if it's also interesting to them.

deltas.product_description:
Should you be open with colleagues about what you earn? In many industries it is still taboo — and in some companies, so-called ‘pay secrecy’ is openly encouraged. 

Discussing money has also long been held as an impolite topic of conversation, but sharing your salary with others can have positive effects. It can help to close wage gaps that exist between genders and minorities at the hiring step, but also when negotiating pay rises. Source:

deltas.product_description:
Why Mahjong is the New LinkedIn for women in Tech

No booths.
No name tags.
Just tiles, stories, laughter, and some serious business ideas.

Yes, Mahjong.
The game you may remember made famous from the movie Crazy rich Asians ?

It’s making a massive comeback, especially among women in tech and AI.
Why?
Because we are looking for more than cold intros and badge scans.
We want real conversations.
We want to connect, collaborate, and build, together.

Here’s what’s happening right now:
Mahjong events are up 180% across the U.S. (Eventbrite data)
San Francisco, LA, and Austin are leading the movement
Founders are replacing VC mixers with Mahjong Nights
Startups are hosting warehouse tournaments with disco balls (!)

And the best part?
There’s no pitch deck required.
Just curiosity, strategy, and room to be yourself.
It’s being called the “IRL LinkedIn for women.”
And honestly, it works.

As someone who speaks on AI, builds in the Bay Area, and shows up for Women in Technology (WIT) and Women in AI communities…
This feels like the future of networking:
Warm, playful, smart, and deeply human.

So if you're building something big, or just looking to belong, Don’t overlook the mahjong table.

The next investor, co-founder, or collaborator might be sitting right across from you.

Let’s rewrite the rules of networking, one tile at a time.
Who’s down for a game?

#WomenInTech #MahjongMovement #AILeadership #GrannyCoreIsPowerCore #BayAreaTech #SisterhoodAndStrategy

Yesterday marked the final day of my Explore (SWE + TPM) Internship at Xbox! 

This summer was an incredible journey of learning and growth. I joined the Experiences and Platforms for Developers: Creator Onboarding, Resources, and Support (XPD-CORS) team, contributing to a greenfield project aimed at transforming how Xbox supports creators in play testing their games.

As a TPM, I led partner studio discussions, authored a feature spec, and collaborated across time zones with a designer to produce Figma designs.

As a SWE, I designed, built, and deployed the backend service, API, and data layer for an end-to-end proof of concept, successfully demoing it to XPD multiple times.

Other highlights included placing 3rd in the Intern Celebration Hackathon, earning 2nd in Halo Reach: Firefight during the Xbox Intern Gaming Tournament, winning a tour of Mojang Studios!

I also played a ton of Sand Volleyball and started reading One Piece

I’m deeply grateful to those who made this internship unforgettable:

deltas.product_description:
“How do some people do so much in one life?"

My six-year-old asked me this during a 30-minute Waymo ride. Instead of talking about productivity, time management, and other loaded concepts, I pulled out my favorite Hindi poem “Koshish karne walon ki kabhi haar nahi hoti.” Those who try never really lose.

It starts with the tiny ant, dragging a grain up a wall. It keeps slipping and falling, but its inner belief fills it with courage, so it returns to the same wall again and again. The effort looks small, but the lesson is huge: if you keep moving, the wall eventually runs out of height. Then a diver, taking repeated dips into the ocean and coming back with empty hands, because pearls do not sit in shallow water. They keep going deeper, knowing that some rewards only appear for people who can handle many empty returns. 

The poem emphasizes perseverance and continuous effort. Failure is a challenge; accept it. See what was lacking and improve it. Give up some comfort, but do not run away from the battlefield of struggle. 

As we reached our destination, I pointed to the Waymo itself. If humanity had not embraced this mindset, we might have stopped at walking. No carts, no cars, no planes, no electric vehicles, and certainly no driverless ride where we can spend 30 minutes on poetry instead of traffic.

To bring it home for him, I reminded him of our hospitality journey. Despite working full time, his parents (Rahil & I) strive to grow our hospitality business Kēsar Vineyards and Events to new heights, not because it is easy but without effort, we will not discover what is possible for our family or the guests we want to serve.

I left him with a question: what is one area you have thought “not for me” that deserves one more honest try this month? Treat every stumble as the ant’s slip or the diver’s empty hand, not as the final verdict.
#growthmindset #grit #parenting #entrepreneurship #hospitality #learning #resilience

While it was a bit of a mixed bag—some iterations didn’t compile and I ended up making manual tweaks—this experience highlighted a few key takeaways:

- **Exploration**: AI surfaced APIs I hadn't encountered before, which was a huge time saver.
- **Refactoring**: Expect to clean up the code. AI can help, but it’s not a substitute for good practices.
- **Learning**: The real value came when I took the time to understand the new concepts introduced.

AI is a powerful tool in the early stages of problem-solving, but nothing beats the clarity that comes from understanding what 'good' looks like. How have others found AI fitting into their workflows? #AI #Coding #WPF #SoftwareEngineering

deltas.product_description:
I'm a little late to the party, but I finally put AI to the test on a real coding project.

The project is a support tool for our development team, so low risk if something went wrong. I asked Claude to improve some WPF canvas code by adding an animated antenna to represent signals from an offshore vessel, along with click events.

It took several iterations. Sometimes the code didn’t compile, sometimes I fixed the bugs myself, and sometimes I just made tweaks by hand (which was faster than re-prompting AI). In the end, the antenna looked good. The code was a bit messy, but then again, the original codebase wasn't exactly pretty either, so maybe the AI was just following our conventions. 

My takeaways from this first round of AI-assisted coding:

- Great for exploration. It surfaced APIs and language features I hadn’t seen before.

- Not great for polish. Expect to refactor if you care about clean code.

- Less useful when you already know the solution. Typing has never been the bottleneck.

The biggest time saver was discovering parts of the WPF Canvas API I didn’t know. Normally, I’d have learned this more slowly through searches and documentation. Still, I believe it's important to take time to study those new concepts that AI introduces us to. Otherwise we aren't really qualified to evaluate the quality of what AI produces.

For me, AI fit neatly into the early phase of problem-solving: piecing together a solution while expanding my knowledge. Once I understood the problem and knew what "good" looked like, it was easier to take over, clean things up, and bring the code up to company standards. Once the solution became clear, prompting AI further felt less like engineering and more like pulling a slot machine lever.

I know I still have a lot to learn in this new programming paradigm. How does this line up with your experience?

I had an interview at Compaq where they were 40 minutes late and one interviewer was pretty insulting with his questions. Like you, I was taught to smile and just get through it. I did, however, sharply correct him on his dismissal of my experience. They called me back and wanted to o hire me but needed me to fill out some preliminary forms.. However, I wasn't keen on working there after that interview. So, I returned their favor for keeping me waiting 40 minutes and said, sure, I'll get you that information to you! I never responded back.

deltas.product_description:
The worst interviewer I ever had:

He swaggered into the room and sat down heavily in his chair. “I just have one question for you,” were his first words. “What’s the difference between Java and C++?”

I described some differences, like garbage collection and generics, and the fact that Java is a proprietary language whereas C++ is an ANSI standard, Java compilers generate bytecode, whereas C++ compilers usually generate native machine code, and so on.

“WRONG!” he roared. “There’s no difference! They’re exactly the same language!” Then he sat back, obviously satisfied at having demonstrated his superiority.

I wanted to ask him, “What did you think you would learn about me as a candidate by asking that question?”

Or, “If I accept a job at this company, would I ever have to talk to you again?”

But I just nodded pleasantly and waited for him to pass me to the next interviewer. I did not complain to the company about him. What would be the point? Did I hope to persuade the company to fire him somehow? If I still wanted the job (which is doubtful), would complaining about an interviewer be the best way to make that happen?

If you have a bad feeling about an interviewer, assume that working for that person will be terrible. Politely finish the interview and never look back.

Complaining about an arrogant interviewer has virtually no upside for you, and is much more likely to demonstrate that you’re just as big an asshole as the interviewer was.

deltas.product_description:
Virtual Network Routing Appliance ("Routing Appliance") Public Preview!

During Ignite, I posted about exciting developments in building a high-performance, native appliance in Azure that will help customers achieve low-latency, high-throughput routing without the need of a Network Virtual Appliance (NVA). Today I'm happy to announce that we have released a public preview of this feature. I hope you get a chance to try it out and we are looking to build many more exciting capabilities on top of this new technology.

NVAs have been the backbone of complex Azure networking topologies where customers needed to route traffic through a central point. While there are still many use cases for NVAs, such as advanced firewalls, for many scenarios NVAs just added complexity but were required to provide a route point. NVAs require the deployment of a virtual machine, route traffic through the guest OS, require the additional overhead of OS management and can create a bottleneck in terms of performance.

Today, with the announcement of the Routing Appliance you get configurable "Azure native" route point with networking speeds up to 200Gbps. You get the management ease of any Azure resource. No need to deploy or configure an operating system. With just a few clicks and an Azure resource deployment, you have a high scale route enabled appliance with native support for Network Security Groups (NSGs) and User Defined Routes (UDRs) giving you easy traffic filtering and routing.

Read More: 

Thanks to Ashish Bhargava, Mario Lopez, Mohamed Hassan, Jackson Crewe, Nitin Goel, Pan Wang, Anshu Verma, Arjun Patel, Rishabh Tewari, Neeraj Motwani, Avijit Gupta, Damini Singal, Michal Zygmunt, Deepak Bansal

#azure #azurenetworking #microsoft

I'm thrilled to announce that the Network Security Perimeter (NSP) is now generally available! 🎉 Working alongside the NSP team has been a rewarding experience, as we've collaborated closely to enhance Azure Virtual Network Manager. NSP provides a critical layer of security by defining logical isolation boundaries for PaaS resources, ensuring that public access is tightly controlled. 

Key features include:
- Resource communication within the perimeter to prevent unauthorized data access.
- Explicit rules for managing external access to PaaS resources.
- Comprehensive access logs for compliance and auditing.
- A unified experience across PaaS resources.

This is a significant step forward in our journey to empower organizations with robust security solutions. Kudos to the entire NSP team for their hard work and dedication! Let's continue to innovate and drive impactful solutions together. #Azure #NetworkSecurity #CloudComputing

deltas.product_description:
As I said in a previous post, today is exciting to have 2 great announcements. This is the second one, where I am so excited to see my sibling team release Network Security Perimeter (NSP) into General Availability! I have had the great privilege to work with this amazing team as they have built a complimentary feature to Azure Virtual Network Manager in the PaaS space. While not my direct team or product, NSP team is my closest peer and I've had the great opportunity to work with them on their design, share code, and build great ideas together. It's a great day to celebrate their achievement and bringing an amazing product to the Azure Portfolio.

Network security perimeter allows organizations to define a logical network isolation boundary for PaaS resources (for example, Azure Storage account and SQL Database server) that are deployed outside your organization’s virtual networks. It restricts public network access to PaaS resources within the perimeter; access can be exempted by using explicit access rules for public inbound and outbound. 

Features of a network security perimeter include: 
* Resource to resource access communication within perimeter members, preventing data exfiltration to non-authorized destinations. 
* External public access management with explicit rules for PaaS resources associated with the perimeter. 
* Access logs for audit and compliance. 
* Unified experience across PaaS resources. 

Learn more about NSP: 

One great example of using NSP is with Azure Monitor:

actual.review_text: Ognjen Sobajic s druge strane, opet da ne bude da ga samo kudimo, uvek mi se svidjalo kako ocas posla napravi dobru prezentaciju gde je sve odlicno objasnjeno sa veoma lepim vizualizacijama. Verovatno je koristio neke napredne AI tool-ove jos tada.

deltas.product_description:
5 dokaza da je Rajder iz Patrolnih šapa toksični mikromenadžer:

1. Ne vrednuje tuđe vreme. Na sastanke saziva sve članove tima, umesto samo one koji će učestvovati u akciji.

2. Govori drugima šta da rade, umesto da njima to prepusti. Kao da Rabl ne zna šta treba da radi kašikom bagera ili Skaj kukom helikoptera.

3. Favorizuje određene članove tima. U ogromnom broju akcija učestvuju Čejs i Skaj, dok Maršal dođe na red u svakoj 10. epizodi (čekali smo pet dana da čujemo koja je Maršalova rečenica kad ga Rajder prozove)

4. Ne poštuje slobodno vreme članova svog tima. Često ih zove čak i po mraku, očekujući da se oni momentalno odazovu. To stvara ogroman stres kod Šapa.

5. Daje klijentima obećanja bez prethodne saglasnosti članova svog tima. Svakom ko ga nazove kaže da odmah dolaze i da će biti rešeno, čime Šapama ne ostavlja nimalo prostora za pregovore i stalno ih baca u vatru.

(Do temperature je, nije do mene)

actual.review_text: The output I pasted is actually based when max_length=15, but even with smaller numbers it can look pretty good.

deltas.product_description:
I would have never thought that creating a simple text prediction software is that easy. After watching one of the Veritasium videos on Markov chains I got inspired by the story how Markov himself created manual and tedios "network" based on the text of Eugene Onegin by Pushkin. I was suspicious that such a simple model with just checking last few charachers can produce text that, of course being non-sensical, seems very legit on the first glance.

So, I've spent an hour of so implementing this in C++, fed it with some English texts and I've got some really decent results, for example, looking like this:

"That wild yell seemed to come a violent puff of wind, and the door to the winding stair blew to with a shock that set the dust from the lintels flying. When I ran to push it open, I found that it was hopelessly fast. I was again a prisoner, and that if I wished it I could have no choice. The Count saw his victory in my bow, and his mastery in the trouble of my face, for he said almost joyously:, you don't count now; the Master is at hand. The attendant thinks it is some sudden form of religious mania at once might be angerous. I got a ladder myself, and crossing the wall, dropped down on the other side of a true love episode of which I know one side already...."

The C++ code is as simple as this 

Anyways, real LLMs are way more complex and they base their predicitons on words, or more preciely tokens and have multiple layers of decision makings. However, a simple code like this can still produce interesting results.

deltas.product_description:
We’re seeing this a lot in the automation space, and honestly, I’ve been guilty of it too.

Most automation agencies make one mistake, they try to sell automation.
But selling automation often adds cost before it adds value.

Because in the early stages, most of what automation promises can actually be done manually, faster, cheaper, and with more context.

The real need for automation begins when manual work becomes the bottleneck, when scaling becomes painful, repetitive, and error-prone.

That’s when automation stops being a “nice-to-have” and becomes an enabler of growth.

So instead of trying to sell automation, we build it where the pain is real, where teams are already doing the work, and scaling it manually no longer makes sense.

At CodingRippler, that’s how we think about automation,
not as a product to push, but as a capability that emerges naturally when scaling requires it.

Maybe automation shouldn’t be sold, it should be discovered.

deltas.product_description:
We’re seeing this a lot in the automation space, and honestly, I’ve been guilty of it too.

Most automation agencies make one mistake, they try to sell automation.
But selling automation often adds cost before it adds value.

Because in the early stages, most of what automation promises can actually be done manually, faster, cheaper, and with more context.

The real need for automation begins when manual work becomes the bottleneck, when scaling becomes painful, repetitive, and error-prone.

That’s when automation stops being a “nice-to-have” and becomes an enabler of growth.

🚨 Did you know that 85% of AI projects fail to deliver value? This statistic hits home for me, especially when I think about Air Canada's costly experience with their chatbot providing incorrect information. It’s a stark reminder of the importance of proper evaluation frameworks. 

Many organizations are still treating LLMs like static databases, which is a fundamental mistake. From my experience building neural control systems for stroke recovery and cancer patient triage, I've seen how crucial it is to evaluate these models as adaptive systems. 

In my latest deep dive, I discuss why traditional accuracy testing often falls short for LLMs and how we can rethink our evaluation frameworks to align with the dynamic nature of AI. The framework you choose today could be the difference between joining the 85% that fail or the 15% that succeed. 

Are you deploying LLMs in your organization? Let’s connect and explore how to ensure your project delivers real value! 

#AIStrategy #EnterpriseAI #RiskManagement #ContinuousLearning #TechLeadership

deltas.product_description:
Change is Coming: The Army Fitness Test Evolves

Starting June 1, 2025, the Army is implementing the new Army Fitness Test (AFT) - a five-event assessment built to meet the demands of modern warfighting.

This isn’t just about removing the standing power throw or renaming the ACFT. It’s about raising the bar for readiness, lethality, and shared standards.

Key changes:
• AFT becomes the official test of record
• 5 events: Deadlift, Push-Up, Sprint-Drag-Carry, Plank, 2-Mile Run
• Sex-neutral, age-normed standards for 21 combat MOSs
• Min score: 350 total / 60 per event (combat MOSs)
• Min score: 300 total (combat-support MOSs)

This change reinforces our commitment to excellence - regardless of rank, MOS, or gender.

We lead with standards.

Are you ready to meet the challenge?

#ArmyFitnessTest #WarfighterStandards #AJOC #ArmyStrong #JuniorOfficers #CombatReady #WarriorEthos #Lethality #Readiness 

The Army Leader Exchange The Center for Junior Officers Kris Saling US Army

Today marks a significant milestone—5 years at Microsoft! 🎉 Reflecting on my journey since graduating from WVU in 2014 with a Bachelor of Arts in Criminology, I am filled with gratitude for the opportunities that have shaped my career. 

I want to extend a heartfelt thanks to GEICO’s Veterans IT Development Program for launching my programming career and to Deloitte for instilling the importance of collaboration and inclusivity in the workplace. 

A special shoutout to the West Virginia Army National Guard for their incredible tuition assistance programs, which allowed me to earn my master's degree debt-free. My experiences as a commissioned Field Artillery Officer have taught me invaluable lessons in leadership and resilience that no classroom could replicate. 

To all the mentors and leaders—both civilian and military—who have guided me along the way, your support has profoundly impacted my life. I am truly grateful for your wisdom and encouragement. 

This journey is a testament to hard work, clear goals, and the power of mentorship. Here’s to the next chapter! 

#Microsoft #SoftwareEngineering #Gratitude #VeteransInTech #Mentorship

actual.review_text: Jeffery Galbraith people like you and the Army taught me a lot. Forced me to be better.

deltas.product_description:
Celebrating 5 Years at Microsoft 

If you had told me when I graduated from WVU in 2014 with a Bachelor of Arts in Criminology that I would one day be a Senior Software Engineer at Microsoft with a Master of Science in Software Engineering, I might not have believed you. Yet here I am in 2025, reflecting on an incredible journey filled with growth, opportunity, and gratitude.

First, I owe so much to GEICO’s Veterans IT Development Program, which gave me my start in programming and changed the trajectory of my career. A heartfelt thanks also to Deloitte, where I learned invaluable soft skills and the true power of an inclusive and collaborative organization.

I’m deeply grateful to the West Virginia Army National Guard for enabling me to earn my master’s degree debt-free through their exceptional tuition assistance programs. If you’re looking for the best deal in education, this is it!

My time as a commissioned Field Artillery Officer in the Army National Guard has taught me leadership and resilience in ways that no classroom or corporate training ever could.

To all the incredible civilian and military leaders and mentors I’ve had the privilege of learning from over the years—your guidance and support have truly shaped who I am today. Your impact on my life has been profound, and I am forever grateful.

This journey is a testament to the power of hard work and having clear goals. It truly is the American dream.

I’m incredibly thankful, humbled, and blessed to be where I am today. Here’s to the next chapter!

#Microsoft #SoftwareEngineering #ArmyNationalGuard #Gratitude #AmericanDream

If you had told me when I graduated from WVU in 2014 with a Bachelor of Arts in Criminology that I would one day be a Senior Software Engineer at Microsoft with a Master of Science in Software Engineering, I might not have believed you. Yet here I am in 2025, reflecting on an incredible journey filled with growth, opportunity, and gratitude.

First, I owe so much to GEICO’s Veterans IT Development Program, which gave me my start in programming and changed the trajectory of my career. A heartfelt thanks also to Deloitte, where I learned invaluable soft skills and the true power of an inclusive and collaborative organization.

I’m deeply grateful to the West Virginia Army National Guard for enabling me to earn my master’s degree debt-free through their exceptional tuition assistance programs. If you’re looking for the best deal in education, this is it!

My time as a commissioned Field Artillery Officer in the Army National Guard has taught me leadership and resilience in ways that no classroom or corporate training ever could.

To all the incredible civilian and military leaders and mentors I’ve had the privilege of learning from over the years—your guidance and support have truly shaped who I am today. Your impact on my life has been profound, and I am forever grateful.

This journey is a testament to the power of hard work and having clear goals. It truly is the American dream.

I’m incredibly thankful, humbled, and blessed to be where I am today. Here’s to the next chapter!

#Microsoft #SoftwareEngineering #ArmyNationalGuard #Gratitude #AmericanDream

prediction.review_text: I'm excited to share that I recently negotiated a $115K increase in my total compensation! 🎉 This experience has highlighted the importance of advocating for ourselves and recognizing our value in the tech industry. It's not just about the figures; it's about the impact we create within our teams and the contributions we make to our projects. I encourage everyone to engage in these discussions and seek mentorship—building meaningful connections can empower us to navigate our careers effectively and achieve fair compensation. Let's lift each other up in our journeys towards professional growth and recognition! 💪 #negotiation #compensation #mentorship #careerdevelopment

actual.review_text: David Urbizo Shawn Pratt 5 square 155 is about 100 pounds...RAP ARE 125ish. Fucking copperhead are monstrous and require 2 people at 200 pounds. Sustained direct rates requires 5 rounds per minute for 3 minutes. That's 1500 pounds over 3 minutes or more technically... a fuck ton of suck

deltas.product_description:
Change is Coming: The Army Fitness Test Evolves

Starting June 1, 2025, the Army is implementing the new Army Fitness Test (AFT) - a five-event assessment built to meet the demands of modern warfighting.

This isn’t just about removing the standing power throw or renaming the ACFT. It’s about raising the bar for readiness, lethality, and shared standards.

Key changes:
• AFT becomes the official test of record
• 5 events: Deadlift, Push-Up, Sprint-Drag-Carry, Plank, 2-Mile Run
• Sex-neutral, age-normed standards for 21 combat MOSs
• Min score: 350 total / 60 per event (combat MOSs)
• Min score: 300 total (combat-support MOSs)

This change reinforces our commitment to excellence - regardless of rank, MOS, or gender.

We lead with standards.

Are you ready to meet the challenge?

#ArmyFitnessTest #WarfighterStandards #AJOC #ArmyStrong #JuniorOfficers #CombatReady #WarriorEthos #Lethality #Readiness 

The Army Leader Exchange The Center for Junior Officers Kris Saling US Army

Change is Coming: The Army Fitness Test Evolves

Starting June 1, 2025, the Army is implementing the new Army Fitness Test (AFT) - a five-event assessment built to meet the demands of modern warfighting.

This isn’t just about removing the standing power throw or renaming the ACFT. It’s about raising the bar for readiness, lethality, and shared standards.

Key changes:
• AFT becomes the official test of record
• 5 events: Deadlift, Push-Up, Sprint-Drag-Carry, Plank, 2-Mile Run
• Sex-neutral, age-normed standards for 21 combat MOSs
• Min score: 350 total / 60 per event (combat MOSs)
• Min score: 300 total (combat-support MOSs)

This change reinforces our commitment to excellence - regardless of rank, MOS, or gender.

We lead with standards.

Are you ready to meet the challenge?

#ArmyFitnessTest #WarfighterStandards #AJOC #ArmyStrong #JuniorOfficers #CombatReady #WarriorEthos #Lethality #Readiness 

The Army Leader Exchange The Center for Junior Officers Kris Saling US Army

Today marks a significant milestone—5 years at Microsoft! 🎉 As I reflect on my journey since graduating from WVU in 2014 with a Bachelor of Arts in Criminology, I am filled with immense gratitude for the opportunities that have shaped my career and the incredible mentors who have guided me along the way.

I owe a huge debt of gratitude to GEICO’s Veterans IT Development Program for launching my programming career. It was a pivotal moment that set me on this incredible path. I also want to thank Deloitte for teaching me the true power of collaboration and inclusivity in the workplace, which has been invaluable in my growth.

A special shoutout to the West Virginia Army National Guard for their exceptional tuition assistance programs that allowed me to earn my master's degree debt-free. My experiences as a commissioned Field Artillery Officer have instilled in me lessons of leadership and resilience that no classroom could replicate.

To all the mentors and leaders—both civilian and military—who have supported me throughout this journey, your guidance has profoundly impacted my life. I am truly grateful for your wisdom and encouragement. 

This journey is a testament to hard work, clear goals, and the transformative power of mentorship. Here’s to the next chapter and to all who are navigating their own paths—never underestimate the impact of a mentor! 

#Microsoft #SoftwareEngineering #Gratitude #VeteransInTech #Mentorship

deltas.product_description:
Celebrating 5 Years at Microsoft 

If you had told me when I graduated from WVU in 2014 with a Bachelor of Arts in Criminology that I would one day be a Senior Software Engineer at Microsoft with a Master of Science in Software Engineering, I might not have believed you. Yet here I am in 2025, reflecting on an incredible journey filled with growth, opportunity, and gratitude.

First, I owe so much to GEICO’s Veterans IT Development Program, which gave me my start in programming and changed the trajectory of my career. A heartfelt thanks also to Deloitte, where I learned invaluable soft skills and the true power of an inclusive and collaborative organization.

I’m deeply grateful to the West Virginia Army National Guard for enabling me to earn my master’s degree debt-free through their exceptional tuition assistance programs. If you’re looking for the best deal in education, this is it!

My time as a commissioned Field Artillery Officer in the Army National Guard has taught me leadership and resilience in ways that no classroom or corporate training ever could.

To all the incredible civilian and military leaders and mentors I’ve had the privilege of learning from over the years—your guidance and support have truly shaped who I am today. Your impact on my life has been profound, and I am forever grateful.

This journey is a testament to the power of hard work and having clear goals. It truly is the American dream.

I’m incredibly thankful, humbled, and blessed to be where I am today. Here’s to the next chapter!

#Microsoft #SoftwareEngineering #ArmyNationalGuard #Gratitude #AmericanDream

prediction.review_text:
I've been reflecting on the concept of 'unit' in unit testing, especially as we navigate the complexities of refactoring. It's fascinating how the definition has shifted from a focus on meaningful behavior to often being tied to classes or methods. 🤔 

I believe we should be testing at the level of functional behavior—grouping classes that deliver capabilities together. This approach not only protects us during refactoring but also keeps our tests relevant and effective. 

I'm genuinely curious about your experiences with this. What patterns have worked for your teams, especially at scale? Let's share insights and help each other refine our testing strategies! #UnitTesting #TDD #SoftwareDevelopment

actual.review_text:
My experience has been that the role of unit tests is to both model the real-world scenarios you'd encounter in production and to test the framework. If a change to the framework occurs you need to change the tests too, those should be fairly straightforward and simple, and safe to regenerate with AI. They provide the least value, guarding only against minor changes that don't alter function signatures or ifaces, they're there to help you when someone changed a label, added an if to some internals, etc.

Situations where "unit" is what should be used as placeholder the meaning of functionality, which may sometimes require some mocking of other subsystems external to your code in order to keep focus on the outcome you're trying to model, are far more valuable and require good separation of concerns.
My exp has been that the minimal API-mocking ones are easy to implement and usually get the time they require, while the latter (which ironically provide far better value) get deferred and may get attention when someone that spent 2 months on some complicated investigation decides to invest that time without approval. I don't really care what people call unit, but I care that the functional tests exist. And a lot of times they don't.

deltas.product_description:
A question for my network — genuinely curious about your experience.

First, let me be clear where I stand: I'm a believer in the full testing pyramid. Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, contract testing, property-based testing, mutation testing, performance testing, resilience testing, chaos engineering — all of it. Each layer catches different things, and I've seen the value at every level.

My question is narrower. It's about what we mean by "unit" in unit testing.

To be fair, I see value in the class-level, mock-heavy approach — especially for coordination layers where you genuinely care that the right services get called in the right order. It's also useful as a design feedback tool: painful mock setup often signals architectural problems. I'm not proposing we abandon it. I'm proposing we layer it with behavior-level testing. Ideally, I'd want both — full coverage at every layer of the pyramid, including both styles of unit testing. The only tests I'd skip are the truly redundant ones (I once saw a team unit test a pure delegation layer that was already exercised by every other layer — that's just noise).

Saying this, I'm not sure the way most teams do unit testing actually helps when it matters most (as a non-regression safety net during refactoring/upgrades that is there to support & encourage such ongoing refactoring).

Here's what I mean. The moment I need tests the most is during refactoring — when I'm restructuring code but want behavior to stay the same. Tests should be my safety net.

But if my tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, then refactoring *breaks the tests*. Not because behavior changed, but because the internal wiring changed. Now I'm rewriting tests and code at the same time, and I've lost my independent witness. I'm not refactoring anymore — I'm rewriting with hope.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.

As we work to support diverse learners, insights from this certification will be invaluable. I'm grateful for the opportunity to grow in this field and would love to connect with anyone interested in trauma-informed practices in education. Let's share resources and insights! #EdTech #LifelongLearning #AdaptiveLearning

prediction.review_text:
As I wrap up my time at solo.io, I want to take a moment to express my heartfelt gratitude for the incredible journey over the past two years. I've had the privilege of diving deep into complex open-source projects like Istio, and I owe a huge thanks to Lin Sun for being an exceptional mentor and guiding me through this experience. Your support has been invaluable, and I truly respect the impact you've made in the open-source community. 🌟

Solo.io is in a fantastic position with its innovative K8S gateway and mTLS solutions, and I’m excited to see where the future takes them. 

Now, I’m thrilled to announce my next chapter at Edera, where I’ll be tackling the critical gaps in K8S workload isolation. Let’s continue to push the boundaries of what’s possible in cloud-native tech together! 💪 #Kubernetes #OpenSource #CloudNative

actual.review_text: It was fantastic to work with you in the Istio community Ben! Best of luck at Edera

deltas.product_description:
This week marks my last at solo.io - it's been a great two years, I've learned a *ton*, enjoyed working with some very smart people, accepted a maintainership for a relatively mature and complex open-source project (Istio), gotten to do lots of interesting low-level Linux stuff, developed a very strong appreciation for both Rust/Tokio, and new respect for *anyone* that takes on the challenge of writing spec-complete ` implementations :P

Extreme shoutout to Lin Sun who gave me this (and many other) opportunities and has been an incredible mentor and boss - Lin is a massive asset to the open-source community as a whole, and someone I genuinely respect and trust on a personal level as well.

solo.io is in a remarkably strong position in the market now - the most powerful OSS K8S gateway offering (kgateway) + the fastest, cheapest and most invisible way to secure cluster traffic with mTLS (Istio Ambient) - and a ton of great people.

It's been great working with you all, I'm off to Edera to help plug the well-known gaps in K8S workload isolation!

Now I'm rewriting tests and code at the same time, and I've lost my independent witness. I'm not refactoring anymore — I'm rewriting with hope.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.

actual.review_text: It was fantastic to work with you in the Istio community Ben! Best of luck at Edera

deltas.product_description:
This week marks my last at solo.io - it's been a great two years, I've learned a *ton*, enjoyed working with some very smart people, accepted a maintainership for a relatively mature and complex open-source project (Istio), gotten to do lots of interesting low-level Linux stuff, developed a very strong appreciation for both Rust/Tokio, and new respect for *anyone* that takes on the challenge of writing spec-complete ` implementations :P

Extreme shoutout to Lin Sun who gave me this (and many other) opportunities and has been an incredible mentor and boss - Lin is a massive asset to the open-source community as a whole, and someone I genuinely respect and trust on a personal level as well.

solo.io is in a remarkably strong position in the market now - the most powerful OSS K8S gateway offering (kgateway) + the fastest, cheapest and most invisible way to secure cluster traffic with mTLS (Istio Ambient) - and a ton of great people.

It's been great working with you all, I'm off to Edera to help plug the well-known gaps in K8S workload isolation!

prediction.review_text:
I've been reflecting on the evolving definition of 'unit' in unit testing, especially as we face the complexities of refactoring. It's intriguing how the focus has shifted from meaningful behavior to often being tied to classes or methods. 🤔 

I firmly believe in the full testing pyramid: unit tests, integration tests, and everything in between. However, I propose we rethink our approach to unit testing. Instead of focusing solely on class-level tests, let's consider testing at the level of functional behavior—grouping classes that deliver capabilities together. This not only safeguards us during refactoring but also keeps our tests relevant and effective. 

During refactoring, tests should act as a safety net, not a cage. If our tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, we risk breaking them during internal changes, which defeats their purpose. I've found that testing behavior, rather than internal implementation, allows for smoother refactoring. 

I'm genuinely curious about your experiences with this. Have you navigated the tension between traditional unit testing and behavior-driven approaches? What patterns have worked for your teams, especially at scale? Let's share insights and refine our testing strategies together! #UnitTesting #TDD #SoftwareDevelopment

actual.review_text:
My experience has been that the role of unit tests is to both model the real-world scenarios you'd encounter in production and to test the framework. If a change to the framework occurs you need to change the tests too, those should be fairly straightforward and simple, and safe to regenerate with AI. They provide the least value, guarding only against minor changes that don't alter function signatures or ifaces, they're there to help you when someone changed a label, added an if to some internals, etc.

Situations where "unit" is what should be used as placeholder the meaning of functionality, which may sometimes require some mocking of other subsystems external to your code in order to keep focus on the outcome you're trying to model, are far more valuable and require good separation of concerns.
My exp has been that the minimal API-mocking ones are easy to implement and usually get the time they require, while the latter (which ironically provide far better value) get deferred and may get attention when someone that spent 2 months on some complicated investigation decides to invest that time without approval. I don't really care what people call unit, but I care that the functional tests exist. And a lot of times they don't.

deltas.product_description:
A question for my network — genuinely curious about your experience.

First, let me be clear where I stand: I'm a believer in the full testing pyramid. Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, contract testing, property-based testing, mutation testing, performance testing, resilience testing, chaos engineering — all of it. Each layer catches different things, and I've seen the value at every level.

My question is narrower. It's about what we mean by "unit" in unit testing.

To be fair, I see value in the class-level, mock-heavy approach — especially for coordination layers where you genuinely care that the right services get called in the right order. It's also useful as a design feedback tool: painful mock setup often signals architectural problems. I'm not proposing we abandon it. I'm proposing we layer it with behavior-level testing. Ideally, I'd want both — full coverage at every layer of the pyramid, including both styles of unit testing. The only tests I'd skip are the truly redundant ones (I once saw a team unit test a pure delegation layer that was already exercised by every other layer — that's just noise).

Saying this, I'm not sure the way most teams do unit testing actually helps when it matters most (as a non-regression safety net during refactoring/upgrades that is there to support & encourage such ongoing refactoring).

Here's what I mean. The moment I need tests the most is during refactoring — when I'm restructuring code but want behavior to stay the same. Tests should be my safety net.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.

prediction.review_text:
I've been reflecting on the evolving definition of 'unit' in unit testing, especially as we face the complexities of refactoring. It's fascinating how the focus has shifted from meaningful behavior to often being tied to classes or methods. 🤔 

I firmly believe in the full testing pyramid: unit tests, integration tests, and everything in between. However, I propose we rethink our approach to unit testing. Instead of focusing solely on class-level tests, let's consider testing at the level of functional behavior—grouping classes that deliver capabilities together. This not only safeguards us during refactoring but also keeps our tests relevant and effective. 

During refactoring, tests should act as a safety net, not a cage. If our tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, we risk breaking them during internal changes, which defeats their purpose. I've found that testing behavior, rather than internal implementation, allows for smoother refactoring. 

I'm genuinely curious about your experiences with this. Have you navigated the tension between traditional unit testing and behavior-driven approaches? What patterns have worked for your teams, especially at scale? Let's share insights and refine our testing strategies together! #UnitTesting #TDD #SoftwareDevelopment

actual.review_text:
My experience has been that the role of unit tests is to both model the real-world scenarios you'd encounter in production and to test the framework. If a change to the framework occurs you need to change the tests too, those should be fairly straightforward and simple, and safe to regenerate with AI. They provide the least value, guarding only against minor changes that don't alter function signatures or ifaces, they're there to help you when someone changed a label, added an if to some internals, etc.

Situations where "unit" is what should be used as placeholder the meaning of functionality, which may sometimes require some mocking of other subsystems external to your code in order to keep focus on the outcome you're trying to model, are far more valuable and require good separation of concerns.
My exp has been that the minimal API-mocking ones are easy to implement and usually get the time they require, while the latter (which ironically provide far better value) get deferred and may get attention when someone that spent 2 months on some complicated investigation decides to invest that time without approval. I don't really care what people call unit, but I care that the functional tests exist. And a lot of times they don't.

deltas.product_description:
A question for my network — genuinely curious about your experience.

First, let me be clear where I stand: I'm a believer in the full testing pyramid. Unit tests, integration tests, end-to-end tests, contract testing, property-based testing, mutation testing, performance testing, resilience testing, chaos engineering — all of it. Each layer catches different things, and I've seen the value at every level.

My question is narrower. It's about what we mean by "unit" in unit testing.

To be fair, I see value in the class-level, mock-heavy approach — especially for coordination layers where you genuinely care that the right services get called in the right order. It's also useful as a design feedback tool: painful mock setup often signals architectural problems. I'm not proposing we abandon it. I'm proposing we layer it with behavior-level testing. Ideally, I'd want both — full coverage at every layer of the pyramid, including both styles of unit testing. The only tests I'd skip are the truly redundant ones (I once saw a team unit test a pure delegation layer that was already exercised by every other layer — that's just noise).

Saying this, I'm not sure the way most teams do unit testing actually helps when it matters most (as a non-regression safety net during refactoring/upgrades that is there to support & encourage such ongoing refactoring).

Here's what I mean. The moment I need tests the most is during refactoring — when I'm restructuring code but want behavior to stay the same. Tests should be my safety net.

But if my tests are written at the class level, mocking every collaborator, then refactoring *breaks the tests*. Not because behavior changed, but because the internal wiring changed. Now I'm rewriting tests and code at the same time, and I've lost my independent witness. I'm not refactoring anymore — I'm rewriting with hope.

The tests became a cage, not a net.

This got me digging into history. When Kent Beck wrote about TDD, a "unit" meant a unit of *behavior* — something meaningful from the perspective of what the system does. Somewhere along the way, particularly as the London school of TDD grew influential, "unit" quietly became "class" or even "method." The vocabulary shifted, and perhaps the intent got lost.

What I've started exploring: testing at the level of functional behavior — a cluster of classes that together deliver a capability. Still unit testing (not integration testing — no real databases, no network calls). Mock at architectural boundaries, not at internal collaboration points. This way, when I refactor the internals, the tests still protect me.

What surprises me is that I hear test architects and senior QA folks talk about this openly, but among developers it's less common in my experience.

Have others navigated this tension? What patterns have worked for your teams — especially at scale? And for those who've studied TDD history more deeply: am I reading this evolution correctly?

Genuinely curious to learn.

deltas.product_description:
This week marks my last at solo.io - it's been a great two years, I've learned a *ton*, enjoyed working with some very smart people, accepted a maintainership for a relatively mature and complex open-source project (Istio), gotten to do lots of interesting low-level Linux stuff, developed a very strong appreciation for both Rust/Tokio, and new respect for *anyone* that takes on the challenge of writing spec-complete ` implementations :P

Extreme shoutout to Lin Sun who gave me this (and many other) opportunities and has been an incredible mentor and boss - Lin is a massive asset to the open-source community as a whole, and someone I genuinely respect and trust on a personal level as well.

solo.io is in a remarkably strong position in the market now - the most powerful OSS K8S gateway offering (kgateway) + the fastest, cheapest and most invisible way to secure cluster traffic with mTLS (Istio Ambient) - and a ton of great people.

It's been great working with you all, I'm off to Edera to help plug the well-known gaps in K8S workload isolation!

prediction.review_text: Should we be open about our salaries? 🤔 In many industries, discussing pay is still considered taboo, yet I believe that salary transparency can be a powerful tool for fostering equity and fairness in our workplaces. As someone who is passionate about inclusive hiring practices, I've seen firsthand how sharing salary information can help close wage gaps and empower individuals during negotiations. 💪 Let's break the silence together! I invite you to vote in our poll and share your thoughts in the comments. How has salary transparency impacted your career journey? #SalaryTransparency #EquityInTech #CareerGrowth

actual.review_text: Transparency should be implemented at company level and it should be presented as brackets, not individual salaries. Based on personal merit and impact on company any individual progresses towards the higher part of the bracket until promoted. The first place to start with this transparency should be in job ads though, many times the lack of this transparency encourages personal statistics made by asking friends who don't really do the job you're applying for or searching inaccurate websites causing many people to make bad decisions of either asking too much or too little in interviews. Transparency can also be a motivating factor, to know what one could earn more by doing some specific type of development can make some people have clear career paths early on, provided they have the dedication, ability and if it's also interesting to them.

deltas.product_description:
Should you be open with colleagues about what you earn? In many industries it is still taboo — and in some companies, so-called ‘pay secrecy’ is openly encouraged. 

Discussing money has also long been held as an impolite topic of conversation, but sharing your salary with others can have positive effects. It can help to close wage gaps that exist between genders and minorities at the hiring step, but also when negotiating pay rises. Source:

prediction.review_text: Should we be open about our salaries? 🤔 This is a topic that often feels taboo in many industries, yet I firmly believe that salary transparency is essential for fostering equity and fairness in our workplaces. In my experience, sharing salary information has been a game-changer, particularly for underrepresented groups during hiring and negotiation processes. It's not just about the numbers; it's about creating an environment where everyone feels valued and informed. Transparency can empower us all to make better decisions and advocate for ourselves effectively. I encourage you to vote in our poll and share your thoughts in the comments. How has salary transparency influenced your career journey? Let's break the silence together! #SalaryTransparency #EquityInTech #CareerGrowth

actual.review_text: Transparency should be implemented at company level and it should be presented as brackets, not individual salaries. Based on personal merit and impact on company any individual progresses towards the higher part of the bracket until promoted. The first place to start with this transparency should be in job ads though, many times the lack of this transparency encourages personal statistics made by asking friends who don't really do the job you're applying for or searching inaccurate websites causing many people to make bad decisions of either asking too much or too little in interviews. Transparency can also be a motivating factor, to know what one could earn more by doing some specific type of development can make some people have clear career paths early on, provided they have the dedication, ability and if it's also interesting to them.

deltas.product_description:
Should you be open with colleagues about what you earn? In many industries it is still taboo — and in some companies, so-called ‘pay secrecy’ is openly encouraged. 

Discussing money has also long been held as an impolite topic of conversation, but sharing your salary with others can have positive effects. It can help to close wage gaps that exist between genders and minorities at the hiring step, but also when negotiating pay rises. Source: 

What do you think about being ‘salary transparent’? Vote in our poll and tell us why in the comments.

I'm excited to share that I've completed the "Become a C++ Developer" learning path on LinkedIn Learning! 🎉 This journey has been a fantastic opportunity to deepen my understanding of C++, particularly through the module "Web Servers and APIs Using C++" by Troy Miles. This module not only transformed my approach to development but also equipped me with practical skills to create and automate web servers, deploy resources using Docker, and integrate external resources like MongoDB. 

As I reflect on this experience, I'm reminded of the importance of continuous learning in our fast-evolving tech landscape. I encourage my fellow developers to embrace learning paths like this one, whether you're just starting out or looking to enhance your skills. Let's support each other in our journeys and keep pushing the boundaries of our knowledge! 

A heartfelt thank you to all the mentors and resources that have guided me along the way. Your support truly makes a difference! 

#developer #docker #programming #cplusplus #objectorientedprogramming #linkedinlearning

actual.review_text: Thank you for the awesome and high quality content! I will for sure watch more courses from you, sir!

deltas.product_description:
I've successfully completed the learning path "Become a C++ Developer" from LinkedIn Learning, a learning path which consists of over 17 hours of C++ theory and in-depth exercises!

My favorite module of the learning path was and still is "Web Servers and APIs Using C++", by Troy Miles.

The module was outstanding, I've learnt how to create and automate Web Servers with C++, deploy resources using Docker, include HTML5, CSS3 and PHP elements and, last but not least, integrate external resources such as MongoDB to the Web Servers.

I'm very grateful for everything that I've learnt and this is just the start!

I recommend the learning path "Become a C++ Developer" for every C++ passionate programmer and for everyone else who would like to enrichen their C++ knowledge.

Link to the LinkedIn Learning Path: 

Thank you, everyone!

actual.review_text: Love this Saritha, I think I need to learn how to play as well. I prefer deep connections and real conversations!

deltas.product_description:
Why Mahjong is the New LinkedIn for women in Tech

No booths.
No name tags.
Just tiles, stories, laughter, and some serious business ideas.

Yes, Mahjong.
The game you may remember made famous from the movie Crazy rich Asians ?

It’s making a massive comeback, especially among women in tech and AI.
Why?
Because we are looking for more than cold intros and badge scans.
We want real conversations.
We want to connect, collaborate, and build, together.

Here’s what’s happening right now:
Mahjong events are up 180% across the U.S. (Eventbrite data)
San Francisco, LA, and Austin are leading the movement
Founders are replacing VC mixers with Mahjong Nights
Startups are hosting warehouse tournaments with disco balls (!)

And the best part?
There’s no pitch deck required.
Just curiosity, strategy, and room to be yourself.
It’s being called the “IRL LinkedIn for women.”
And honestly, it works.

As someone who speaks on AI, builds in the Bay Area, and shows up for Women in Technology (WIT) and Women in AI communities…
This feels like the future of networking:
Warm, playful, smart, and deeply human.

So if you're building something big, or just looking to belong, Don’t overlook the mahjong table.

The next investor, co-founder, or collaborator might be sitting right across from you.

Let’s rewrite the rules of networking, one tile at a time.
Who’s down for a game?

#WomenInTech #MahjongMovement #AILeadership #GrannyCoreIsPowerCore #BayAreaTech #SisterhoodAndStrategy

prediction.review_text: Hoje, ao encerrar um ciclo de 13 anos na Microsoft, não posso deixar de refletir sobre a jornada incrível que vivi. Cada projeto, cada colaboração, e cada desafio me ensinaram lições valiosas e me permitiram contribuir para a história da Microsoft, especialmente na liderança em Inteligência Artificial e na evolução do Azure. Estou profundamente grato a todos os colegas que estiveram ao meu lado, suas contribuições foram essenciais para o sucesso que alcançamos juntos. Fiz amigos para a vida toda e sou grato por cada momento compartilhado. Agora, estou animado para iniciar uma nova jornada que promete trazer experiências complementares ao que vivi até aqui. Fiquem ligados para as novidades que estão por vir! 🚀 #Gratidão #NovosDesafios #Microsoft

actual.review_text: Daniel Semedo, desejo muito sucesso em sua nova jornada. Foi um prazer ter trabalhado com você, sempre solícito, alegre e com grande profissionalismo.

deltas.product_description:
Encerro um ciclo de 13 anos na Microsoft, uma jornada que me trouxe aprendizados imensos, grandes realizações e a oportunidade de viver o sonho de trabalhar em uma das maiores empresas de tecnologia do mundo. Tive experiências que ultrapassaram fronteiras, colaborando com clientes e colegas em projetos no Brasil e no exterior. Foi um privilégio participar na linha de frente de momentos marcantes e escrever uma parte da história da Microsoft, como a liderança em Inteligência Artificial, o fortalecimento e expansão do Azure e a evolução da engenharia de software através do Visual Studio, Azure DevOps e posteriormente com GitHub, impactando centenas de milhares de desenvolvedores ao longo desses anos nos quais tive oportunidade de visitar, conhecer e conversar.

Nada disso teria sido possível sem as pessoas incríveis que estiveram ao meu lado nessa trajetória. Sou profundamente grato a todos que me apoiaram, inspiraram e me ensinaram em cada projeto, conversa e desafio. Além de colegas, fiz amigos para a vida toda e sou grato por cada história vivida juntos.

Chegou o momento de iniciar uma nova jornada, ela será interessante e diferente do que já trabalhei, uma boa mudança que trará experiência complementar de tudo que vivi nesses 13 anos de Microsoft, mas essas novidades deixarei para uma próxima história (post)!

prediction.review_text:
Yesterday marked the final day of my Explore (SWE + TPM) Internship at Xbox! 🎉 This summer has been an incredible journey filled with learning and growth. I had the privilege of joining the XPD-CORS team, where we worked on a greenfield project that aims to transform how Xbox supports creators in play testing their games. 

As a TPM, I led engaging discussions with partner studios and collaborated across time zones with a talented designer to create impactful Figma designs. On the SWE side, I designed, built, and deployed the backend service, API, and data layer for an end-to-end proof of concept, successfully demoing it multiple times to the XPD team. 

A heartfelt thank you to my pod, the ThunderDome, and all the incredible mentors and teammates who made this experience unforgettable. Your guidance and support were invaluable! 

Special shoutouts to:
- Suvir Arora and Alex Blount for your leadership
- Brian Trevethan and Greg Gonyea for your mentorship
- Anthony Keller, Maria Lopez Quesada, Daniel Jones, David Kushmerick, Kyle Sweeney, Jason Williams, Eddie Iglesias III, George Ju, and Ai Fukuda for being amazing teammates

deltas.product_description:
Why Mahjong is the New LinkedIn for women in Tech

No booths.
No name tags.
Just tiles, stories, laughter, and some serious business ideas.

Yes, Mahjong.
The game you may remember made famous from the movie Crazy rich Asians ?

It’s making a massive comeback, especially among women in tech and AI.
Why?
Because we are looking for more than cold intros and badge scans.
We want real conversations.
We want to connect, collaborate, and build, together.

Here’s what’s happening right now:
Mahjong events are up 180% across the U.S. (Eventbrite data)
San Francisco, LA, and Austin are leading the movement
Founders are replacing VC mixers with Mahjong Nights
Startups are hosting warehouse tournaments with disco balls (!)

And the best part?
There’s no pitch deck required.
Just curiosity, strategy, and room to be yourself.
It’s being called the “IRL LinkedIn for women.”
And honestly, it works.

As someone who speaks on AI, builds in the Bay Area, and shows up for Women in Technology (WIT) and Women in AI communities…
This feels like the future of networking:
Warm, playful, smart, and deeply human.

So if you're building something big, or just looking to belong, Don’t overlook the mahjong table.

The next investor, co-founder, or collaborator might be sitting right across from you.

Let’s rewrite the rules of networking, one tile at a time.
Who’s down for a game?

#WomenInTech #MahjongMovement #AILeadership #GrannyCoreIsPowerCore #BayAreaTech #SisterhoodAndStrategy

deltas.product_description:
Encerro um ciclo de 13 anos na Microsoft, uma jornada que me trouxe aprendizados imensos, grandes realizações e a oportunidade de viver o sonho de trabalhar em uma das maiores empresas de tecnologia do mundo. Tive experiências que ultrapassaram fronteiras, colaborando com clientes e colegas em projetos no Brasil e no exterior. Foi um privilégio participar na linha de frente de momentos marcantes e escrever uma parte da história da Microsoft, como a liderança em Inteligência Artificial, o fortalecimento e expansão do Azure e a evolução da engenharia de software através do Visual Studio, Azure DevOps e posteriormente com GitHub, impactando centenas de milhares de desenvolvedores ao longo desses anos nos quais tive oportunidade de visitar, conhecer e conversar.

Nada disso teria sido possível sem as pessoas incríveis que estiveram ao meu lado nessa trajetória. Sou profundamente grato a todos que me apoiaram, inspiraram e me ensinaram em cada projeto, conversa e desafio. Além de colegas, fiz amigos para a vida toda e sou grato por cada história vivida juntos.

Chegou o momento de iniciar uma nova jornada, ela será interessante e diferente do que já trabalhei, uma boa mudança que trará experiência complementar de tudo que vivi nesses 13 anos de Microsoft, mas essas novidades deixarei para uma próxima história (post)!

In my journey as a community builder and mentor, I’ve learned that authentic connections are what truly drive us forward. With Mahjong events surging by 180% across the U.S., it’s clear we’re craving spaces where we can be ourselves and collaborate creatively. 

This playful approach to networking fosters trust and transparency, allowing us to build relationships that matter. It’s about more than just business; it’s about creating a supportive environment where we can thrive together. 

So, who’s ready to join me for a game? Let’s rewrite the rules of networking, one tile at a time. The next investor, co-founder, or collaborator might just be sitting across from you. #WomenInTech #MahjongMovement #CommunityBuilding #AuthenticConnections

deltas.product_description:
Why Mahjong is the New LinkedIn for women in Tech

No booths.
No name tags.
Just tiles, stories, laughter, and some serious business ideas.

Yes, Mahjong.
The game you may remember made famous from the movie Crazy rich Asians ?

It’s making a massive comeback, especially among women in tech and AI.
Why?
Because we are looking for more than cold intros and badge scans.
We want real conversations.
We want to connect, collaborate, and build, together.

Here’s what’s happening right now:
Mahjong events are up 180% across the U.S. (Eventbrite data)
San Francisco, LA, and Austin are leading the movement
Founders are replacing VC mixers with Mahjong Nights
Startups are hosting warehouse tournaments with disco balls (!)

And the best part?
There’s no pitch deck required.
Just curiosity, strategy, and room to be yourself.
It’s being called the “IRL LinkedIn for women.”
And honestly, it works.

As someone who speaks on AI, builds in the Bay Area, and shows up for Women in Technology (WIT) and Women in AI communities…
This feels like the future of networking:
Warm, playful, smart, and deeply human.

So if you're building something big, or just looking to belong, Don’t overlook the mahjong table.

The next investor, co-founder, or collaborator might be sitting right across from you.

Let’s rewrite the rules of networking, one tile at a time.
Who’s down for a game?

#WomenInTech #MahjongMovement #AILeadership #GrannyCoreIsPowerCore #BayAreaTech #SisterhoodAndStrategy

deltas.product_description:
Encerro um ciclo de 13 anos na Microsoft, uma jornada que me trouxe aprendizados imensos, grandes realizações e a oportunidade de viver o sonho de trabalhar em uma das maiores empresas de tecnologia do mundo. Tive experiências que ultrapassaram fronteiras, colaborando com clientes e colegas em projetos no Brasil e no exterior. Foi um privilégio participar na linha de frente de momentos marcantes e escrever uma parte da história da Microsoft, como a liderança em Inteligência Artificial, o fortalecimento e expansão do Azure e a evolução da engenharia de software através do Visual Studio, Azure DevOps e posteriormente com GitHub, impactando centenas de milhares de desenvolvedores ao longo desses anos nos quais tive oportunidade de visitar, conhecer e conversar.

Nada disso teria sido possível sem as pessoas incríveis que estiveram ao meu lado nessa trajetória. Sou profundamente grato a todos que me apoiaram, inspiraram e me ensinaram em cada projeto, conversa e desafio. Além de colegas, fiz amigos para a vida toda e sou grato por cada história vivida juntos.

Chegou o momento de iniciar uma nova jornada, ela será interessante e diferente do que já trabalhei, uma boa mudança que trará experiência complementar de tudo que vivi nesses 13 anos de Microsoft, mas essas novidades deixarei para uma próxima história (post)!
